title: Monetary Policy Report—October 2025
summary: The Bank of Canada released its October 2025 Monetary Policy Report, coinciding with a 25 basis point rate cut to 2.25%, as the Canadian economy grapples with steep US tariffs, sluggish exports, and subdued business investment. The Bank projects Canadian economic growth will strengthen from approximately 0.75% in H2 2025 to average 1.4% annually over 2026-2027, while maintaining inflation near the 2% target over the projection horizon.
key_points: Bank of Canada reduced its overnight rate target by 25 bps to 2.25% (Bank Rate at 2.5%, deposit rate at 2.20%)
CPI inflation was 2.4% in September 2025, up from approximately 2% in prior months; underlying inflation remains around 2.5%
Exports and business investment have slumped due to US tariffs and elevated uncertainty
Canada GDP growth projected at roughly 0.75% in H2 2025, then averaging 1.4% annually for 2026-2027
The Bank characterizes the trade conflict as having a lasting negative impact on Canada's economic activity
Shifts in cost pressures from US tariffs and global trade reconfiguration are creating upward pressure on business input costs
why_it_matters: This quarterly monetary policy report provides the official baseline for Canada's inflation outlook and growth trajectory amid an active US tariff regime. The 25 bps rate cut signals the Bank's willingness to ease policy to support a slowing economy even as tariff-driven cost pressures persist. Market participants and analysts will use the projections and risk assessments to calibrate positioning in CAD, Canadian bonds, and trade-sensitive equities.
macro_context: The Bank of Canada faces a delicate balancing act: the tariff shock is suppressing growth and exports, warranting accommodation, while simultaneously pushing up business costs, keeping underlying inflation above the 2% target. The projections embed a baseline assumption that US tariff policy remains in flux with new trade agreements being signed, yet the Bank explicitly warns the trade situation poses elevated risk to the outlook. The reconfiguration of global supply chains is expected to create persistent cost pressures even as growth recovers.
